summary:
To allow for natural tooth defense through secondary dentin formation and prevent premature cavity expansion during initial management phases when full removal isn't necessary due to intact dental structure. The approach supports both short- and long-term health outcomes without compromising future restoration options if needed later based on disease progress monitoring. Agent1's summary focuses more directly on addressing your specific query about why an incomplete debridement might be performed specifically after identifying it was intended within the context provided earlier where "deeper" referred explicitly to deeper decay rather than just any level beyond surface involvement. Here are two concise sentences capturing essential aspects related solely back towards this particular scenario involving deep caries requiring partial cleaning prior to final filling procedures:

Incomplete debridement allows time for potential self-repair processes like new dentine growth which can help stabilize severe but not yet fully collapsed areas of enamel loss from extensive decay. This method preserves healthy tissue around affected regions until complete resolution occurs naturally or additional conservative treatments become appropriate depending upon follow-up assessments indicating stability has been achieved sufficiently enough so no urgent intervention would otherwise disrupt healing pathways established thus far under controlled conditions post-debriding procedure implementation initially targeting only partially degraded zones identified clinically beforehand.
